titleEn: Poultry WorldPartnerCase study: Transforming hatchery operations with WingScanJul 7Video
titleZh: WingScan自动性别鉴定系统：意大利Capetta孵化场从10人手工到4人智能化的转型实践
summaryZh: 意大利Capetta孵化场每周生产48万只雏鸡，传统手工性别鉴定面临劳动力短缺、效率低、准确性差和动物福利问题。引入WingScan自动化系统后，每小时处理量从1.5-1.6万只提升至3.5-4万只，人工从10人减至4-5人，工作时间从12小时缩短至6小时，同时提高了鉴定一致性和雏鸡福利。该案例展示了AI视觉与自动化在禽业孵化环节的落地价值，为行业提供了可复用的转型路径。
featuredReason: 该案例完整呈现了AI视觉+自动化在禽业孵化环节的落地效果，数据详实（效率提升2倍+、人工减少50%+、工时减半），对面临劳动力短缺的禽企具有直接借鉴意义，是智慧畜牧中“少人化、高精度”转型的典型范例。
contentFull: Case study: Transforming hatchery operations with WingScanCapetta Hatchery in Neive, Italy, which is owned and managed by Sergio Capetta, produces around 480,000 chicks each week, equating to approximately 24 million chicks annually. The business had been relying on traditional manual chick sexing, a process that was becoming increasingly difficult to sustain due to labour shortages and inconsistent results. Jul 7VideoWatch the video below to see how Capetta Hatchery transformed its chick sexing process with WingScan. Manual sexing presented serious operational challenges. Skilled labour was becoming harder to find, especially for a role that requires precision and is often performed at unusual hours. “The job of the sexer is a very particular job,” explains Capetta. “Staff can no longer be found who are suited to do the job well.”The process was also labour intensive and inefficient. Around 10 workers were needed on the line, often working shifts lasting up to 12 hours. Even with this level of staffing, productivity remained limited and accuracy could vary as workers became fatigued.Manual handling raised additional concerns about chick welfare. Chicks were handled multiple times during the process, increasing stress and the risk of trauma.Faced with these pressures, Capetta saw change as essential... SolutionCapetta Hatchery implemented WingScan, an automated chick sexing system designed to increase throughput, improve accuracy, and reduce reliance on manual labour while enhancing animal welfare.On installation, Capetta said: “For my business, there is no future without this machine. In a few years you won’t find anyone doing manual sexing anymore.”ImplementationThe introduction of WingScan had a huge impact. Instead of 10 workers carrying out manual sexing, the operation now runs efficiently with 4-5 people. One person loads crates onto the conveyor while 3 operators place chicks onto the line, allowing the machine to run continuously.Hatchery Manager Domenico Torre described the new setup: “With 4 or 5 people, we can process a large number of chicks. The machine keeps running continuously at a steady pace.”The system proved easy to adopt. Operators were able to quickly understand how to use it, thanks to its intuitive interface. As WingScan operator Erik Sarotto noted: “Interacting with the machine is very easy because it’s very intuitive – you immediately know where to act.”ResultsProductivity and labour efficiencyThe most immediate impact was a significant increase in throughput. Output rose from approximately 15,000-16,000 chicks per hour to as many as 35,000-40,000 chicks per hour.At the same time, labour requirements dropped. “Before, on the line, there were around 10 people. Now we have 1 person,” said Torre, referring specifically to the role of manual sexing.Working hours were reduced by half. “Before the working day lasted 12 hours; now it is 6 hours, with the same numbers of chicks,” said Capetta.Accuracy and consistencyWingScan delivered a major improvement in sexing accuracy. Error rates fell to around 1%. “The sexing now, in my opinion, is perfect with a 1% error,” Capetta explained.This improvement has also been confirmed on farms. Paolo Marenco shared that “there are no longer imbalances between males and females.”Animal welfare improvementsReducing manual handling has had a clear positive impact on chick welfare. Previously, chicks were handled multiple times throughout the process. With WingScan, that has been reduced to a single transfer step.Marenco highlighted the difference: “Chicks experience much less trauma than before. Now there is only 1 step, so the chicks are handled much less.”Operators and customers have observed improvements in chick quality and lower mortality rates. Sarotto added: “They’re handled much less. There has been a complete improvement in every area.”Operational reliability and ease of useOperators consistently report that the system is simple and reliable. The interface provides clear, real-time feedback on performance and alerts users to issues before they escalate.Sarotto explained: “The machine alerts you to an error before it stops. Intervention is quite immediate.”Most issues are minor and are resolved in seconds, often with simple cleaning. The system also captures detailed production data, allowing operators to monitor sex ratios, unclassified chicks, and machine performance throughout the day.Marenco summarised the experience: “Everything is completely accessible in a very, very simple way. Anyone can use it.”Service and supportSupport from the WingScan team has been proactive and dependable. The installation included training that enabled the team to manage maintenance independently.Marenco noted: “They taught us how to handle maintenance, and they are always present and available for support.” In some cases, issues were identified remotely before production began and were resolved quickly.Capetta reinforced this view: “They respond; sometimes they anticipate us.”OutcomeThe adoption of WingScan has fundamentally improved operations at Capetta Hatchery. The business now operates with higher productivity, greater accuracy, reduced labour dependence, and improved animal welfare outcomes.Reflecting both internal gains and positive feedback from customers, Capetta says: “Everyone has found an improvement. Everyone!”ConclusionBy transitioning to automated sexing with WingScan, Capetta Hatchery has addressed critical labour challenges while achieving measurable gains in efficiency, quality, and welfare. The move has positioned the business for long-term sustainability in an industry where manual sexing is becoming increasingly difficult to maintain.
translationZh:
案例研究：WingScan助力孵化场运营转型

位于意大利内维的Capetta孵化场由Sergio Capetta所有并管理，每周生产约48万只雏鸡，相当于每年约2400万只。该企业一直依赖传统的手工雏鸡性别鉴定，这一过程因劳动力短缺和结果不一致而变得越来越难以维持。

观看下方视频，了解Capetta孵化场如何借助WingScan实现雏鸡性别鉴定流程的转型。

手工性别鉴定带来了严峻的运营挑战。熟练劳动力越来越难找，尤其是对于这种需要精准操作且常在非正常时段进行的工作。“性别鉴定员是一份非常特殊的工作，”Capetta解释道。“已经找不到适合做好这份工作的员工了。”

该过程还劳动密集且效率低下。生产线上需要约10名工人，通常轮班工作长达12小时。即使有这么多人手，生产力仍然有限，而且随着工人疲劳，准确性也会出现波动。

手工操作还引发了对雏鸡福利的额外担忧。雏鸡在过程中被多次处理，增加了应激和受伤的风险。

面对这些压力，Capetta认为变革势在必行……

**解决方案**

Capetta孵化场引入了WingScan，这是一套自动化雏鸡性别鉴定系统，旨在提高吞吐量、提升准确性、减少对人工的依赖，同时改善动物福利。

在安装时，Capetta表示：“对我的企业来说，没有这台机器就没有未来。几年后，你将找不到任何人再做手工性别鉴定。”

**实施效果**

WingScan的引入带来了巨大影响。原本需要10名工人进行手工性别鉴定，现在只需4-5人即可高效运行。一人负责将雏鸡箱放到传送带上，三名操作员将雏鸡放置到生产线上，使机器能够连续运行。

孵化场经理Domenico Torre描述了新的工作模式：“只需4-5人，我们就可以处理大量雏鸡。机器以稳定的速度持续运行。”

该系统易于上手。得益于其直观的界面，操作员能够快速掌握使用方法。正如WingScan操作员Erik Sarotto所指出的：“与机器交互非常容易，因为它非常直观——你立刻就知道该在哪里操作。”

**成果**

生产力与劳动效率

最直接的影响是吞吐量显著提升。产量从每小时约1.5万至1.6万只雏鸡提高到每小时3.5万至4万只。

与此同时，劳动力需求大幅下降。“以前，生产线上大约有10个人。现在只需要1个人，”Torre在专门提到手工性别鉴定岗位时说道。

工作时间减少了一半。“以前工作日长达12小时；现在只需6小时，处理相同数量的雏鸡，”Capetta说。

准确性与一致性

WingScan系统
author: Targan
